meta.title: TeacherAI Sections
meta.description: Configurable sections for the canvas, defined via a simple JSON DSL.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Geometric interpretation of quadratic and cubic equations

sections.title: Equation (1)
sections.content:
$x^2 = mx + n.$
(1)

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: From elementary school we have learned how to find its solutions, that is, all the values of $x$ that satisfy equation (1). To do so we just need to use the widely known quadratic formula

sections.title: Equation (2)
sections.content:
\[ x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a} \]
(2)

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
of the general quadratic equation
$$ax^2 + bx + c = 0$$
(3)

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: By rewriting equation $x^2 - mx - n = 0$ (1) as we can use (2) to obtain

sections.title: Equation (2)
sections.content:
\[ x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a} \]
(4)

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: If we plot equation (1) we can observe geometrically that it represents the intersection of the parabola $y = x^2$ with the line $y = mx + n.$ This can be appreciated in the following applet. Drag the sliders below and observe what happens with the intersection points $x_0$ and $x_1.$

sections.title: Parabola and Line with Sliders
sections.description: y=x^2 and y=mx+n with adjustable m and n, showing intersections

sections.title: GeoGebra: Parabola and Line with Sliders
sections.description: y=x^2 and y=mx+n with sliders m and n; intersection points labeled
sections.content:
{
  "mode": "applet",
  "app": "graphing",
  "params": { "showMenuBar": false, "showToolBar": false, "showAlgebraInput": false, "showZoomButtons": true },
  "height": 440,
  "commands": [
    "m = 0.3",
    "n = 2",
    "f(x) = x^2",
    "g(x) = m*x + n",
    "x0 = Intersect(f, g, 1)",
    "x1 = Intersect(f, g, 2)",
    "ShowLabel(x0, true)",
    "ShowLabel(x1, true)",
    "SetPointSize(x0, 6)",
    "SetPointSize(x1, 6)",
    "SetColor(f, 199, 68, 64)",
    "SetLineThickness(f, 6)",
    "SetColor(g, 64, 64, 64)"
  ]
}
